13: A direct revelation: 'One like a son of man came with the clouds of heaven and came to the Ancient of Days and was presented before him. And to him was given dominion and glory and a kingdom, that all peoples, nations, and languages should serve him; his dominion is an everlasting dominion, which shall not pass away, and his kingdom one that shall not be destroyed.' Jesus's consistent self-identification as 'the Son of Man' throughout the Gospels is a deliberate claim to be this figure — the one who receives from the Ancient of Days the universal, eternal dominion. The ascension is the receiving of this dominion; Pentecost is the beginning of its exercise; the parousia is its final manifestation. The 'Son of Man' claim is Jesus's most characteristic and most Christologically loaded self-designation.
1: A shadow: Daniel 7 is the christological apex of the OT — the chapter that most directly names the one who will receive universal, eternal dominion. The setting: the first year of Belshazzar, before the fall of Babylon. The vision comes to an exile through dream — the medium through which God addressed Joseph. The humility of the recipient frames the magnitude of the revelation.
2: A shadow: "Four winds of heaven stirring up the great sea." The sea in prophetic literature is the realm of chaos and death (Isaiah 57:20; Revelation 21:1 "there was no more sea"). The four winds driving the chaos are the forces of historical turbulence from which human empire rises. The Son of Man comes "with the clouds of heaven" (Daniel 7:13) — from above the chaos. His origin contrasts with the beasts' from the beginning.
3: A shadow: "Four great beasts came up from the sea, each different from the others." The four beasts are the succession of world empires — the same kingdoms as the statue of chapter 2, seen from below (as beasts) rather than from above (as precious metals). The progression of empires is the problem the Son of Man's kingdom solves. Each beast rises, dominates, and is superseded; the Son of Man's kingdom alone is "everlasting" (Daniel 7:14).
4: A shadow: The first beast — lion with eagle's wings, given a human mind — is Babylon. Its humanization anticipates Nebuchadnezzar's own in Daniel 4. Empire that operates on purely bestial terms eventually receives enough human dignity to recognize its limits. The trajectory is toward acknowledgment of the Most High — which is also the trajectory of all creation toward Christ (Philippians 2:10-11).
5: A shadow: The second beast — bear raised on one side, three ribs in its mouth, "devour much flesh" — is Medo-Persia. The progressive escalation of destructive power through the beasts is the OT's honest assessment of what human empire does — the problem that Christ's kingdom displaces (1 Corinthians 15:24-25: "then comes the end, when he delivers the kingdom to God after destroying every rule and authority and power").
6: A shadow: The leopard with four wings — Greece and Alexander's rapid conquest — moves with supernatural speed. The four heads represent the division of Alexander's empire after his death. The pattern: rapid rise, fragmentation, dissolution. Every human empire that seems to achieve universality splinters. The contrast with the Son of Man's undivided, eternal dominion (Daniel 7:14) is deliberate.
7: A shadow: The fourth beast — "terrifying, dreadful, and overpowering... different from all the beasts that preceded it, and it had ten horns" — deliberately transcends any single historical empire. It represents the final form of human power organized against God. The NT uses this imagery for the Roman imperial cult (Revelation 13:1-8); its "ten horns" become the background for John's beast.
8: A shadow: The little horn — speaking boastful words, with "eyes like human eyes and a mouth speaking great things" — is the prototype of Paul's "man of lawlessness" (2 Thessalonians 2:3-4) and John's "beast" (Revelation 13:5-6). Christ's return is specifically presented as the termination of this figure (2 Thessalonians 2:8: "the Lord Jesus will kill him with the breath of his mouth").
9: A revelation of God: "Thrones were set in place and the Ancient of Days took his seat. His clothing was white as snow and the hair of his head like pure wool. His throne was flaming fire and its wheels were blazing fire." The Ancient of Days is the eternal Judge. John's vision of the risen Christ in Revelation 1:14 gives him the same white hair, linking the glorified Christ to this imagery — but the scene of Daniel 7:9 and Daniel 7:13 distinguishes the Son of Man approaching the Ancient of Days. Father and Son are both present; the scene is Trinitarian in structure even in its OT form.
10: A theme: "A river of fire flowed out from before him; thousands upon thousands attended him, and myriads upon myriads stood before him; the court sat in judgment and the books were opened." The heavenly court scene is the template for the great white throne judgment (Revelation 20:11-15) and the scene of Hebrews 12:22-24 (the assembly of the firstborn enrolled in heaven). Christ will preside at this judgment as both Son of Man and righteous Judge (John 5:27: "he has given him authority to execute judgment because he is the Son of Man").
11: A shadow: "Then the beast was killed and its body destroyed and given over to be burned with fire." The destruction of the fourth beast in response to the boastful horn anticipates Revelation 19:20 (the beast and false prophet thrown into the lake of fire). Christ's return consigns the final empire to permanent destruction — the fire that kills the beast is the fire that precedes the Ancient of Days (Daniel 7:10).
12: A shadow: "As for the rest of the beasts, their dominion was taken away, but their lives were prolonged for a season and a time." Previous empires stripped of ultimate power but permitted to continue — Babylon (Nebuchadnezzar restored), Persia (Cyrus as God's anointed, Isaiah 45:1), Greece (preserved as culture). This is the pattern of common grace: empires under sovereign providence that awaits the full disclosure of the Son of Man's kingdom.
14: A direct fulfillment: "He was given authority, glory, and sovereign power. All peoples, nations, and languages worshiped him. His dominion is an everlasting dominion that will not pass away, and his kingdom is one that will never be destroyed." This is the explicit NT claim for the resurrected Christ (Matthew 28:18: "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me"; Philippians 2:9-11: "every knee should bow... to the glory of God the Father"). Jesus applied Daniel 7:13-14 to himself before the high priest (Matthew 26:64). The "all peoples, nations, and languages" formula used of every empire in Daniel here transfers to the Son of Man as its only legitimate possessor.
15: A theme: "My spirit was troubled within me and the visions of my head dismayed me." The prophet's response to overwhelming revelation — trembling, confusion, needing help to stand — is the pattern of encounter with divine glory (Isaiah 6:5; Ezekiel 1:28; Revelation 1:17). Daniel's bewilderment at a vision he cannot fully understand mirrors the disciples' bewilderment at the transfiguration and resurrection appearances.
16: A theme: Daniel approaches one of those standing nearby and asks for the true meaning. The desire to understand what the vision truly signifies is the posture of prophetic humility. The Spirit's role in the NT is to interpret what Christ has accomplished (John 16:13-14: "he will take what is mine and declare it to you") — the same movement of heaven providing interpretation to the overwhelmed human recipient.
17: A shadow: "These four great beasts are four kings who will rise from the earth." Their origin "from the earth" contrasts with the Son of Man's origin "with the clouds of heaven" (Daniel 7:13). The christological point: the Son of Man is the heavenly answer to the earthly problem. He does not arise from the sea of chaos; he comes from above it.
18: A direct promise: "But the holy ones of the Most High will receive the kingdom and will possess it forever — yes, forever and ever." The saints' inheritance of the kingdom is the corollary of the Son of Man's reception of dominion. In Christ, believers are made co-heirs (Romans 8:17); in Christ, the church will reign with him (Revelation 2:26-27; Daniel 20:4; 1 Corinthians 6:2-3). Daniel 7:18 is the OT foundation for the NT's theology of shared eschatological inheritance.
19: A shadow: Daniel asks about the fourth beast — "the truth about" it. The persistent desire to understand the mystery of the final empire and its destruction is the posture of the church under persecution. NT epistles repeatedly address communities suffering under Rome's fourth-beast power, pointing them to the certainty of the horn's destruction (Revelation 13:10; 2 Thessalonians 1:5-10).
20: A shadow: The little horn that defeats others, "which had eyes and a mouth that spoke boastfully and which seemed greater in appearance than its companions." The self-exalting king who defeats rivals is applied in the NT to both the Roman imperial cult (Revelation 13:5-6) and the "man of lawlessness" (2 Thessalonians 2:4). Christ is the one who unmasks and ends this entire category of power.
21: A shadow: "This horn was waging war against the holy ones and prevailing over them." The temporary apparent victory of the persecutor over the saints is the pattern addressed throughout the NT (Revelation 12:17; Daniel 13:7: "the beast was allowed to make war on the saints and to conquer them"). The tribulation is real, and for a time the enemy prevails. But Daniel 7:22 is the answer: "until the Ancient of Days came."
22: A direct promise: "Until the Ancient of Days came and pronounced judgment in favor of the holy ones of the Most High, and the time came when the holy ones possessed the kingdom." The entire period of the little horn's apparent victory is bounded by the single word "until." The Ancient of Days arrives; judgment is pronounced in favor of the saints. This is the plot of Revelation: the saints under persecution, the Lamb returns, all things are made new.
23: A shadow: The fourth beast as a fourth kingdom that "will devour the whole earth and trample it down and crush it." The comprehensive destructive ambition of the final empire is the backdrop against which the Son of Man's universal, constructive dominion is displayed. Where the beast devours, the Son of Man gives life; where the beast crushes, the Son of Man lifts up.
24: A shadow: The ten horns and the different king who subdues three and sets himself against the Most High. The escalating self-deification of the final ruler is the trajectory Paul traces in 2 Thessalonians 2:3-10: the son of destruction "opposes and exalts himself against every so-called god or object of worship, so that he takes his seat in the temple of God, proclaiming himself to be God."
25: A direct shadow: "He will speak words against the Most High and will wear down the holy ones of the Most High. He will try to change the sacred seasons and the law; and they will be handed over to him for a time, times, and half a time." Revelation 12:14 quotes "a time, times, and half a time" directly as the duration of the dragon's persecution. The fixed duration is the NT's assurance that the persecution is bounded: it will end precisely when God decrees.
26: A direct promise: "But the court will sit in judgment, and his dominion will be taken away — utterly consumed and destroyed forever." The final destruction of the anti-kingdom is absolute and permanent. Paul's "utterly consumed and destroyed" in 2 Thessalonians 2:8 echoes this verse directly. Christ's return is not merely a defeat of the enemy but a termination of the entire system of opposition to God.
27: A direct fulfillment: "The sovereignty, power, and greatness of all the kingdoms under heaven will be handed over to the people of the Most High. His kingdom will be an everlasting kingdom, and all rulers will worship and obey him." Revelation 11:15 presents this as accomplished: "The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he will reign forever and ever." Daniel 7:27 is the OT promise; Revelation 11:15 is the eschatological announcement of its fulfillment in Christ.
28: A theme: "This is the end of the account. As for me, Daniel, my thoughts greatly troubled me and my color changed, but I kept the matter in my heart." The prophet who has received the definitive vision of the Son of Man's kingdom keeps it in his heart — unable yet to speak it fully. Revelation 22:10 directly inverts Daniel's sealing command: "Do not seal up the words of the prophecy of this book, for the time is near." What Daniel must keep sealed, the church proclaims openly — because the fulfillment has come in Christ.
